title: Database Access on Informix

Database Access on Informix

Database Access on Informix

Open/Close Support

The open and close ProcScript statements are fully supported.

When using the single-connection mechanism, closing a logon path causes all paths through the connector to be closed. If you end a transaction on a logon path, that transaction is ended on all paths accessed by the connector.

Database Connections

Informix can make connections to several database environments at the same time, and establish multiple connections to the same database environment, as long as each connection has a unique connection name. The name of the connection consists of the database name concatenated with a unique dbhandle_id identifier. The syntax is as follows:

DatabaseName dbhandle_id

An application can only interact with a database through a current connection. Only one connection can be current at any one time; other connections are dormant. A connection becomes current when a new connection is established, or when an existing connection is reestablished, that is, making a dormant connection current.

Multiple concurrent transactions are supported, in which each connection can have its own transaction. Global transactions—in which a single transaction spans databases over multiple connections—are not supported by Informix.

The Informix connector allows you to access tables in several databases simultaneously by making use of Informix connections and Informix table-naming conventions. When generating SQL statements, the connector prefixes the table name with the database environment used in the specified path.

The Informix connector can support the following connection mechanisms:

  • Single-connection mechanism—only the first logon request after start-up or after a logoff request is accepted. The specified user ID and password are ignored and the connection remains current until disconnected.
  • Multiple-connection mechanism—every logon request is translated into a connection. The user ID and password are optional.
  • Connection managed by a Transaction Manager (TM)—not supported in this release. For more information on TM controlling global transactions, see your specific TM documentation.

To set the connection mechanism, use the connection connector option. For more information on the Informix connection mechanism, see your Informix documentation.

Logon Path

When specifying the logon path, you must supply a database environment. A database environment can consist of a database, or a database and a database server. For Informix, specify the following:

DatabaseName{@ServerName}

Password and user ID are ignored for the single-connection mechanism, and are optional for the multiple-connection mechanism.

Single-Connection Mechanism

The connection is made using the following SQL statement:

connect to DatabaseEnv as ConnectionName

Subsequent logon requests to databases only verify whether the specified database can be reached, by preparing the following SQL statement:

select 0 from DatabaseEnv :informix.systables where 1 = 0

If this fails, the logon fails.

With a logoff request, a disconnection is performed on the first connection of the database. Subsequent logoff requests will therefore have no effect. The disconnection is made with the following SQL statement:

disconnect ConnectionName

SQL DDL statements can only be executed on the current connection—in this case, the first database connected. This means that you cannot create, drop, or modify an object on any database other than on the first connected database.

You can access multiple databases through one connection. You can specify up to 16 different logon paths for different database environments, and up to 16 different logon paths per database environment (logon aliases), using Uniface assignment files. This means that a maximum of 16 x 16 logon paths are possible at any one time. For example, the following example specifies three different paths:

$INF1 INF:db1@db1server||
$INF1_ALIAS INF:db1@db1server||
$INF2 INF:db2@db2server||

$INF1_ALIAS is a logon alias of $INF1. Remember, however, that only the first connection requested by Uniface to Informix is actually made.

Multiple-Connection Mechanism

The connections are made using the following SQL statement:

connect to DatabaseEnv as ConnectionName {user UserName using Password } with concurrent transaction

To execute SQL statements on a connection path, the connection is made current using the following SQL statement:

set connection ConnectionName

This makes all other connections dormant.

With a logoff request, disconnections are made using the following SQL statement:

disconnect ConnectionName

Multiple connections are possible with Informix. However, if the Informix shared-memory connection mechanism is used, only one connection is possible. By using Uniface assignment files, you can specify up to 16 different logon paths for different database environments (including user IDs and passwords) and up to 16 different logon paths per database environment (logon aliases). This means that a maximum of 16 x 16 connections are possible at one time. For example:

$INF1 INF:db1@db1server||
$INF1_ALIAS INF:db1@db1server||
$INF2 INF:db2@db2server|foo|foo
$INF2_SECOND INF:db2@db2server|foo2|foo2
$INF3 INF:db3@db3server|foo3|foo3

$INF1_ALIAS is a logon alias of $INF1. Connections are made for each path. In the example above, five connections are made. However, $INF2_SECOND is not a logon alias because it has a different user ID and password.

Concurrent Transactions

Multiple-concurrent transactions are only supported with the multiple-connection mechanism.

CAUTION: If multiple connections with concurrent transactions are assigned to the same database environment, a deadlock condition can occur.

Informix Logging

It is not possible to access two or more Informix databases with a mixture of logging and no logging at the same time. The type of logging for different databases, however, can be ‘mixed’ (buffered, unbuffered, and ANSI-mode logging).

Related Links
  • Database Access

    Uniface automatically opens the DBMS when a read or write ProcScript statement is issued. The path and logon details for the specific database (or databases) can be provided in an assignment file, a command line instruction, or in the open ProcScript instruction.

  • Paths and Path Assignments

    A path is a named route to a resource required by the application, such as a database connector and database, a remote server, or a file location.

Was this topic helpful?
LikeDislike
